title: Ruth: A Theme of Covenant
speaker: Elder Scott Hollander
source_files: Ruth_ A Theme of Covenant - Elder Scott Hollander.txt

outline.title: I. Background: Ruth in Its Historical and Literary Context
subpoints: Ruth is a historical narrative — a Hebrew short story — set during the period of the judges, probably during the time of Gideon (1192-1152 BC)
The refrain of Judges applies: in those days there was no king in Israel; everyone did what was right in his own eyes
The book is covenantal: themes of redemption, hesed (loving kindness), and providence
Bethlehem (house of bread) is now a house of famine — a telling irony
Elimelech means 'my God is King' — significant in a time when Israel had no king

outline.title: II. Key Covenantal Terms Defined
subpoints: Redemption: buying something back at a cost; the kinsman redeemer had both obligation and opportunity to buy back a relative's estate
Levirate marriage (Deuteronomy 25:5-10): a childless widow's brother-in-law must marry her to raise up an heir for the deceased husband
Hesed: Hebrew word translated 'loving kindness,' 'faithfulness,' or 'mercy' — an enduring, steadfast, covenantal love that will not fail
Providence: God's hand working through ordinary people, moving His covenantal plan forward

outline.title: III. Chapter 1: Grief Upon Grief and Covenant Faithfulness
subpoints: Naomi loses husband and both sons — without an heir, Elimelech's household loses its inheritance
Naomi's name means 'pleasant' — in chapter 1 she renames herself 'Mara' (bitter), misinterpreting God's providence
Ruth's declaration of covenant loyalty mirrors Abraham's call — she leaves country and family to follow Yahweh
Naomi prayed for Yahweh's hesed; Ruth demonstrated it in her response: wherever you go I will go

outline.title: IV. Chapter 2: Yahweh's Providence in Ordinary Life
subpoints: She happened to come to the part of the field belonging to Boaz — Hebrew idiom using 'chance' to point to the hand of God
Boaz is immediately characterized as a covenantal man, greeting his workers with the blessing of Yahweh
Boaz addresses Ruth as 'daughter,' not as property — an act of covenantal grace toward a foreigner
Boaz attributes his generosity to the hesed Ruth demonstrated to Naomi; he blesses her with Yahweh's hesed
Naomi awakens from depression when she hears the name Boaz: 'He is one of our redeemers'

outline.title: V. Chapters 3-4: The Kinsman Redeemer Acts
subpoints: Chapter 3: Naomi and Ruth demonstrate hesed toward each other; Ruth confronts Boaz with his duty as redeemer
Boaz responds with hesed: all that you say I will do — a covenant response pointing forward to Christ
Chapter 4: Poloni Almoni (the unnamed near-kinsman, 'such and such') refuses when the cost is too high
Boaz redeems and marries Ruth; Obed is born, declared the son of Naomi, restoring the family line
The genealogy at the close points to David — and ultimately to Jesus, our blessed Kinsman Redeemer

outline.title: VI. Ruth Points to Christ the Kinsman Redeemer
subpoints: Boaz is a type of Christ — he intervened for his family when no one else could, restored lost possessions, took Ruth as his bride when she was destitute
Simeon holding the infant Jesus in Luke 2 mirrors Naomi holding Obed — both are pictures of salvation arriving
The seed of the earth (grain) and the seed of the woman (Obed) both reverse the famine of chapter 1
Yeshua means salvation — the name of Jesus connects to the redemption theme of Ruth
Ruth's genealogy in Matthew and Luke places Boaz in the lineage of the Messiah

summary:
Elder Scott Hollander surveys the entire book of Ruth in a single lesson, organizing it around the three covenantal themes of redemption, hesed (loving kindness), and providence. The lesson begins by placing Ruth in its historical context — the chaotic period of the judges when everyone did what was right in their own eyes — and defining key covenantal terms: the kinsman redeemer's obligation to buy back a relative's estate, levirate marriage, and the rich Hebrew word hesed, which carries connotations of steadfast, promise-keeping, relational love. Students are encouraged to add hesed to their working theological vocabulary.

Hollander traces the plot of all four chapters, noting how Yahweh's hand moves through the ordinary decisions of Ruth, Naomi, and Boaz. The narrative's use of 'chance' in chapter 2 — Ruth happening to come to Boaz's field — is identified as a Hebrew device pointing to divine providence. Boaz's covenantal character is highlighted throughout: he greets his workers with Yahweh's blessing, addresses Ruth as 'daughter,' and ultimately acts as the kinsman redeemer when the unnamed relative (called Poloni Almoni — 'such and such') refuses the cost. The lesson closes by drawing the typological connections forward: Boaz foreshadows Christ, who intervened for His family when no one else could, at the highest possible cost. The birth of Obed echoes proleptically in Simeon holding the infant Jesus, and the genealogy leads directly to David and to the Messiah.
transcript:
## Introduction

So this morning, we'll be looking at Ruth. How many of you remembered to read ahead? Fair enough — one person did this week.

So as we look at Ruth today, we'll be looking at the themes of covenant. Ruth is a covenantal book, and the themes of covenant that we'll be looking at are redemption, loving kindness, and providence. Lord willing, we'll look at Psalm 2 next week.

The book of Ruth is a historic book — a historic narrative, also called a Hebrew short story. That means it is a historically accurate account of real events recounted to the reader in narrative form. Believers and unbelievers alike have described it as a masterful composition, and it is a beautiful account.

We're going to go over the whole book today, all four chapters, but we don't have time to read the text of four whole chapters. So we'll be covering the theme and hitting a lot of the highlights. Hopefully this will prompt you to read it this week and then you'll have some thoughts running around in the back of your head as you're reading.

But this beautiful account is not about romantic love. It's about covenantal love, covenantal redemption, covenantal providence in ordinary life, as well as covenantal providence foreshadowed in the Kinsman Redeemer. We see it first in Boaz, but then also pointing to our blessed Kinsman Redeemer, Jesus Christ.

So you can open your Bible to Ruth or your phones or your personal Bible. I'm going to begin by reading Ruth 1:1-5.

## Ruth 1:1-5

Now it came to pass in the days when the judges ruled that there was a famine in the land, and a certain man of Bethlehem Judah went to dwell in the country of Moab, he and his wife and his two sons. The name of the man was Elimelech, the name of his wife was Naomi, and the names of his two sons were Mahlon and Chilion, Ephrathites of Bethlehem Judah. And they went to the country of Moab and remained there. Then Elimelech, Naomi's husband, died, and she was left with her two sons. Now they took wives of the women of Moab, the name of the one was Orpah, and the name of the other Ruth. And they dwelt there about ten years. Then both Mahlon and Chilion also died, so the woman survived her two sons and her husband.

## Historical Setting

Ruth is a historical account and I like to place things in history. As Ruth 1:1 reminded us, the setting takes place during the period of the judges, probably during the time of Gideon — between 1192 and 1152 BC. That's most easily established because of the genealogy at the end of Ruth.

The period of the judges was characterized by a theme repeated twice. Does anyone remember what that theme was? In those days there was no king in Israel; everyone did what was right in his own eyes. Yet the story of Ruth will demonstrate that amid the chaos, God is still in control and working His plan of redemption. And soon, a righteous king — David, a man after God's own heart — would be raised to unite and lead His people. And David would also serve as a type of Jesus, as his great-grandfather Boaz will in this story.

The setting of Ruth takes place in Bethlehem and Moab. And the irony is that Bethlehem means house of bread — and yet now it is the house of famine.

A note on the characters: there is significance that an Israelite would marry a Moabite. The Moabites were descendants of Lot — from an incestuous relationship, as Genesis 19 tells us. The Israelites were treated harshly by the Moabites coming out of Egypt — the King of Moab attempted to have Balaam curse the Israelites. And Moses reminded the people in Deuteronomy that a Moabite was not to enter the assembly of the Lord, even to the tenth generation, because they did not meet them with bread and water on the road.

I did also want to draw attention to the meaning of Elimelech's name. Elimelech means 'my God is King' — poignant because the theme of this period is that there was no king in Israel and everyone did what was right in his own eyes. In the naming of their son, his parents said, we have no king but my God is King. Naomi's name means 'my pleasant one' or 'pleasant.'

## Key Covenantal Terms

The three themes of covenant we'll be addressing are redemption, loving kindness, and providence. Let me quickly define these terms.

Redemption: the act of buying something back — saving someone or something at a cost. The kinsman redeemer was the relative with both the obligation and the opportunity to buy back his relative's estate, or to buy them out of slavery or bankruptcy.

Closely tied to that yet distinct in its own right is levirate marriage. It is described in Deuteronomy 25:5-10. Basically, when a husband dies childless, his brother is supposed to marry the childless widow to give her an heir that is technically the deceased brother's child.

Now regarding loving kindness — let me introduce you to the word hesed. There are several words from the original languages you already know when you hear them used in church. You know shalom, you know Yahweh — the covenantal name of God. When you read 'Lord' in all capitals in your Old Testament, that's what it's referring to. I prefer to say Yahweh distinctly, especially when it's talking about that name in that specific context.

Hesed is one of my favorite Hebrew words because of the deep implications it carries. It's frequently translated in the Old Testament as loving kindness, faithfulness, or mercy. It refers to an enduring faithfulness, a steadfast love, a promise-keeping covenantal love that will not fail. And it has significant undertones of a very deep, relational love. I prefer to think of all the implications wrapped up in hesed rather than tagging one English word to it — and that's why I encourage you to put it in your vocabulary so that all this imagery and covenantal relationship and love and depth flow into that word. Hesed is a beautiful theme echoed throughout the book of Ruth.

Providence: the hand of God working through the lives of ordinary people, moving His covenantal plan forward. And there's a beautiful subplot in this book with the demonstration of Yahweh's sovereignty in seed — first in chapter 1, His provision of grain seed to end the famine, and then at the end of the book in chapter 4, His provision of human seed to address the famine of the soul.

## Chapter 1: Grief Upon Grief

Chapter 1 is the recounting of grief upon grief. Naomi already bearing the grief of being a refugee becomes a widow. Then as a widow she loses her children as well. The commentator Bruce Walkie notes: the first episode features no grain seed; the second, no human seed. Naomi's bereavement is double.

She is too old to have another child or heir. Without an heir, Elimelech's household will lose its inheritance and social immortality in Israel. All that Naomi has left are her two daughters-in-law. Three surviving widows — especially Naomi — are left in desperate need of hesed from someone. If Walkie's words summarize our theme in a sentence: when His covenantal people are utterly helpless to save themselves, He redeems them by demonstrating love and kindness.

We see the covenantal hand of Yahweh begin to move in Ruth 1:6. Yahweh restores the seed of the ground — there's going to be a harvest in Bethlehem — and Naomi hears of it and prepares to return home. She encourages her daughters-in-law to return to their homes and find new husbands. Even in verses 11 through 13, Naomi introduces the covenantal practice of levirate marriage — she's saying, I have no more sons to replace your husbands.

In verses 8 and 9, Naomi had invoked a covenantal prayer for Yahweh's hesed to be demonstrated to them. But then what is Ruth's response? Naomi prayed for Yahweh's blessing — Ruth demonstrated it. She declares: entreat me not to leave you, or to turn back from following after you. For wherever you go, I will go; and wherever you lodge, I will lodge. Your people shall be my people, and your God, my God. Where you die, I will die, and there I will be buried. The Lord do so to me, and more also, if anything but death parts you and me.

And we know that her intention is covenantally minded because she's making a vow before Yahweh.

Ruth's response is a classic expression of true faith, loyalty, and love to God and His people. Walkie notes: like Abraham, Ruth left her country and family to follow Yahweh to an unseen land.

The historical events of Ruth take place during the period of Passover and Pentecost — we know this because Ruth 1 ends with mentioning the barley harvest.

## Chapter 2: Yahweh's Providence

Chapter 2 is a demonstration of Yahweh's providence moving within the normal activities of His people.

Ruth 2:2-3: So Ruth the Moabitess said to Naomi, please let me go to the field and glean heads of grain after him in whose sight I may find favor. And she said to her, go, my daughter. Then she left and went and gleaned in the field after the reapers. She happened to come to the part of the field belonging to Boaz, who was of the family of Elimelech.

Despite the marked subtlety in the phrase 'she happened to come,' it is actually a Hebraism — using the word for chance to point to the hand of God. The commentator Leon Morris explains it this way: she happened to — this translation of the expression makes it clear that Ruth did not understand the full significance of what she was doing. She just went about her life, and then boom — something providential stares her in the face. He says the phrase points to the truth that men do not control events, but that the hand of God was behind them as He works His purpose out.

In verses 1 and 3, the author repeats the information that Boaz was from Elimelech's clan. The hand of God is over Ruth's action, and he does not want us to miss that.

Consider the flow of our theme: when His covenant people are utterly helpless to save themselves, He redeems them by demonstrating love and kindness. Yahweh directed Ruth — unbeknownst to her — into the field of Boaz.

This should be an encouragement to you to search for the hand of providence in your everyday lives — and not just to focus on the blessings. Think of how even the negative things work out, providentially speaking. God even brings the weird and bad things on the surface in our lives for our good. A recent event that happened to us personally: on Easter morning, a box of groceries was stolen off our front porch. We have ring doorbells and cameras everywhere. On any other given day, the camera should have picked it up. But at 5am on Easter morning, a man came up, stole the package, and the doorbell didn't alert us. I say providence — because on any other given day it would have been caught.

As we're introduced in chapter 2 to Boaz, he is immediately demonstrated as a man invoking the blessing of Yahweh over and over again. In verse 4: Boaz came from Bethlehem and said to the reapers, Yahweh be with you. He greets them with the blessing of Yahweh. He is a covenantally-minded man.

Boaz is illustrated as a man who not only invokes the blessing upon the people, but who materializes that blessing. He says to Ruth: do not go to glean in another field, nor go from here. Stay close to my young women. He invoked the blessing of Yahweh even on Ruth, and then he materialized that blessing.

Unlike the men in Judges, Boaz does not address Ruth as a piece of property. Instead, he addresses her as daughter — a covenantally gracious act. And Boaz attributes his generosity to the hesed Ruth demonstrated to Naomi. He blesses her with Yahweh's hesed: Ruth 2:11-12: It has been fully reported to me, all that you have done for your mother-in-law since the death of your husband, and how you have left your father and your mother and the land of your birth and have come to a people you did not know before. Yahweh repay your work, and a full reward be given you by Yahweh God of Israel, under whose wings you have come for refuge.

Note the similarities to Genesis 12:1-2 and the language of Boaz that could be interpreted as an allusion to the coming matriarchal blessing of Ruth.

In the early verses of chapter 2, Naomi seems to be in a stupor of depression — her first observable behavior is inactivity. She was probably in her forties; she's not 80 years old unable to glean. We also observe her lack of direction — she gives no advice to Ruth about going to Boaz's field, merely permission. However, in verse 20 it's like a light switch flips on. She says: Yahweh has not forsaken His hesed to the living and the dead! Boaz may be our redeemer!

## Chapters 3-4: The Kinsman Redeemer Acts

In chapter 3, I want us to consider the practical implications of hesed in the ancient Israelite covenantal community. Yahweh provides ownership of grain left behind by harvesters for the alien, the poor, the widow, and the fatherless. He provides levirate marriage for the childless widow. He also provides a family protector — the kinsman redeemer — for the needy relative.

Naomi, her mother-in-law, said to her: my daughter, should I not seek rest for you, that it may be well with you? Is not Boaz our relative, with whose young women you were?

Naomi is acknowledging both her own obligation of hesed to Ruth, and that Boaz has an obligation to seek their rest as well. Further hesed in covenant community is demonstrated in the words of Ruth and Boaz. Ruth says to Naomi: all that you say I will do — a classic hesed response. In The Princess Bride, what does Wesley say? As you wish — his subtle way of saying, I love you.

Ruth 3:8-9: Now it happened at midnight that the man was startled and turned himself, and there was a woman lying at his feet. He said, who are you? She answered, I am Ruth, your maidservant. Take your maidservant under your wing, for you are a close relative.

Just as Naomi invoked the responsibilities of hesed upon herself with an implication toward Boaz, Ruth is now confronting Boaz: you are my redeemer, redeem me. This is mirrored in Yahweh's demonstration to His covenantal people in Ezekiel 16:8: when I passed by you again and saw you, behold you were at the age of love, and I spread the corner of My garment over you and covered your nakedness; I made My vow to you and entered into a covenant with you.

Likewise, Boaz responded to Ruth in hesed: do not fear; I will do for you all that you request. And this points forward to Christ — because that was Mary's response when the angel told her a child would come to her. Mary said: behold, I am the servant of the Lord; let it be to me according to your word.

Chapter 4 speaks to the actions of the Kinsman Redeemer. The near kinsman in Ruth is actually the foil and anti-hero. The author of Ruth, in order to avoid memoralizing this selfish kinsman, refers to him as Poloni Almoni — both words meaning 'a certain one' or 'someone.' Translated in various Bibles as 'such and such' or 'so and so.' By no stretch is he a friend. And so his name is struck from history.

Walkie says: the near kinsman is willing to participate in the covenant community as long as it involves no risk and no sacrifice. Such action does not reflect the ethics of God's kingdom community. So his name is struck from history, and the spotlight turns to Boaz.

Redemption is a leading theme — the key word redeemed is used 11 times as a verb, 9 times as a noun. Walkie reminds us: contrary to popular opinion, the book of Ruth is not a romantic love story but a story of unselfish and sacrificial love within a family that can survive in no other way. That's what hesed is all about.

In Ruth 4:14, the women of Bethlehem proclaimed: Blessed be the Lord who has not left you this day without a redeemer, and may his name be renowned in Israel. These women were speaking of Boaz — and the name of Boaz points forward to our blessed Kinsman Redeemer, Yeshua. Salvation is His name. He comes to save His people.

Jesus is the blessed Kinsman Redeemer. He intervened for His family when no one else could — restoring our lost possessions, buying us back at a great price, taking us as His bride when we were destitute. That's what Ruth points us to. That's what Boaz points us to, as a type of Christ.

## Conclusion: Yahweh's Providence in the End

The story concludes with the working out of God's providence. In contrast to Ruth 1:20 where Naomi says, 'don't call me pleasant, call me bitter,' Naomi's great-great-grandson, the Psalmist David, would write in Psalm 113:9: He gives the barren woman a home, making her the joyous mother of children. Praise Yahweh.

And Ruth 4:17: the women of Bethlehem gave him a name saying, a son has been born to Naomi. Boaz becomes a husband and father for the first time. Ruth becomes a mother for the first time. Naomi becomes a grandmother for the first time. And even in a legal sense, in the relationship of redemption, Obed is declared the son of Naomi, the progeny of Elimelech.

Another beautiful picture pointing us to Christ: Ruth 4:16 — Naomi took the child and laid him on her bosom and became a nurse to him. It might be a little loose, but I think it's a beautiful picture of when Simeon took up Jesus in his arms in Luke 2:28-32: Lord, now You are letting Your servant depart in peace, according to Your word; for my eyes have seen Your salvation which You have prepared in the presence of all peoples — a light for revelation to the Gentiles and for glory to Your people Israel.

The seed revisited: God alone grants life in the seed of the earth and the seed of the woman. Through the former He initiates Naomi's redemption; through the latter He completes it. Note how the field in Bethlehem lay barren for 10 years, as did Ruth while her husband died without leaving her a child. Yet the story unfolds from famine to plentiful harvest and a royal lineage.

I wish we had time for questions. Let's close in prayer. Our Heavenly Father, we do thank You that You demonstrate Your providence in the lives of Your covenantal community — recorded for us to observe so that we can take comfort in it in our own covenantal communities. We're so thankful for the redemption You've provided to us in Jesus Christ, our Kinsman Redeemer. And we ask Your blessing on our Lord's Day. We ask this in Christ's name. Amen.
